request.question: What Elton John album included a song that was later covered in 2002 by Tim McGraw?

title: Red Rag Top
Angry All the Time
Watch the Wind Blow By
Tim McGraw (song)
Honky Cat
Emotional Traffic
Mona Lisas and Mad Hatters
Tiny Dancer
Tim McGraw and the Dancehall Doctors
One of These Days (Marcus Hummon song)
sentences: "Red Rag Top" is a song written by Jason White and performed by American country music singer Tim McGraw.
It was released in September 2002 as the first single from his album "Tim McGraw and the Dancehall Doctors".
The song peaked at number 5 on the U.S. "Billboard" Hot Country Singles & Tracks chart in early 2003 and peaked at number 40 on the U.S. "Billboard" Hot 100 chart.
"Angry All the Time" is a song written by Bruce Robison and first recorded on his 1998 album "Wrapped".
It was later covered by Tim McGraw with guest vocalist Faith Hill.
Released in July 2001, McGraw's version was the second single from his "Set This Circus Down" album.
The song reached Number One on the "Billboard" Hot Country Singles & Tracks (now Hot Country Songs) chart.
"Watch the Wind Blow By" is a song written by Anders Osborne and Dylan Altman, and performed by American country music singer Tim McGraw.
It was released in October 2003 as the fifth and final single from McGraw's album "Tim McGraw and the Dancehall Doctors".
The song reached the top of the "Billboard" Hot Country Singles & Tracks chart on March 20, 2004.
It also peaked at number 32 on the "Billboard" Hot 100.
"Tim McGraw" is the debut single and first published song recorded by American singer-songwriter Taylor Swift.
The song was written by Swift and Liz Rose, and produced by Nathan Chapman.
It was released on June 19, 2006 by Big Machine Records as Swift's debut single and the lead single from Swift's eponymous debut album.
Swift wrote "Tim McGraw" during her freshman year of high school, knowing that she and her senior boyfriend would break up at the end of the year when he left for college.
The song was written about all the different things that would remind the subject of Swift and their time spent together, once he departed.
"Tim McGraw" is a musical interconnection of traditional and modern country music.
Lyrically, the track lists items in order to associate a past relationship, one of them being country artist Tim McGraw's music.
"Honky Cat" is a song from the 1972 Elton John album "Honky Château," the album's lead-off track.
Emotional Traffic is the eleventh studio album by American country music artist Tim McGraw.
Originally completed in late 2010, it is McGraw's last studio album released by Curb Records, a label he's been with since his debut album "Tim McGraw" (1993).
It was released on January 24, 2012.
"Mona Lisas and Mad Hatters" is a song from the Elton John album "Honky Château".
The lyrics were written by Bernie Taupin and is his take on New York City after hearing a gun go off near his hotel window during his first visit to the city.
The song's lyrics were partly inspired by Ben E. King's "Spanish Harlem," written by Jerry Leiber and Phil Spector, in which he sings "There is a rose in Spanish Harlem."

In response to this, Taupin writes, Allmusic critic Stewart Mason noted that the song is "less saccharine than many similar Elton John and Bernie Taupin ballads" and praised the "somewhat uncharacteristic emotional directness" of its lyrics.
It was released as the B-side of the "Harmony" U.K. single in 1980.
"Tiny Dancer" is a 1971 song by Elton John with lyrics by Bernie Taupin.
It appears on John's fourth album, "Madman Across the Water", and was released as a single in 1972.
In the U.S. it was certified Gold on 19 May 2005, and Platinum on 19 August 2011 by the Recording Industry Association of America.
In the UK, "Tiny Dancer" has been certified Silver by the British Phonographic Industry (BPI) for sales of 200,000 copies, despite never being released as an official single.
Tim McGraw and the Dancehall Doctors is the seventh studio album by country musician Tim McGraw and the first to feature his band The Dancehall Doctors.
It was released in November 2002 and was recorded on a mountaintop studio in upstate New York.
Four singles were released.
Two songs were in the movie "Black Cloud", starring McGraw.
The album also included a cover of Elton John's "Tiny Dancer", which was released only to the AC format, although it also reached the country charts from unsolicited airplay.
The album debuted at number 2 on the Billboard 200 with first week sales of 602,000
"One of These Days" is a song co-written and originally recorded by American country music singer Marcus Hummon on his 1995 album "All in Good Time".
It was later covered by Tim McGraw, whose version is the fourth single from his 1997 album "Everywhere".
It peaked at number two in the United States, and number one in Canada.

title: Tim McGraw and the Dancehall Doctors
Tiny Dancer
Tiny Dancer

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: What Elton John album included a song that was later covered in 2002 by Tim McGraw?
hop1_docs: Tim McGraw and the Dancehall Doctors | Tim McGraw and the Dancehall Doctors is the seventh studio album by country musician Tim McGraw and the first to feature his band The Dancehall Doctors.  It was released in November 2002 and was recorded on a mountaintop studio in upstate New York.  Four singles were released.  Two songs were in the movie "Black Cloud", starring McGraw.  The album also included a cover of Elton John's "Tiny Dancer", which was released only to the AC format, although it also reached the country charts from unsolicited airplay.  The album debuted at number 2 on the Billboard 200 with first week sales of 602,000

Let It Go (Tim McGraw album) | Let It Go is the ninth studio album by Tim McGraw.  Released on March 27, 2007, it was his first studio album in two and a half years.  "Let It Go" entered the U.S. "Billboard" 200 at number one with sales of 325,000.  The album has produced seven Top 20 singles on the "Billboard" Hot Country Songs charts, including a number one; one of those seven songs was only included on later issues of the album.  Of all McGraw's albums, this one has produced the most singles in his career.

Tim McGraw (album) | Tim McGraw is the first album by American country music artist Tim McGraw, released in 1993.  It includes the singles "What Room Was the Holiday In", "Welcome to the Club", "Two Steppin' Mind", and "Memory Lane", none of which reached the Top 40 on the country charts.  This is the only studio album of McGraw's career not to achieve a music recording sales certification or to enter the Top Country Albums charts.

Angry All the Time | "Angry All the Time" is a song written by Bruce Robison and first recorded on his 1998 album "Wrapped".  It was later covered by Tim McGraw with guest vocalist Faith Hill.  Released in July 2001, McGraw's version was the second single from his "Set This Circus Down" album.  The song reached Number One on the "Billboard" Hot Country Singles & Tracks (now Hot Country Songs) chart.
Elton John and Tim Rice's Aida | Elton John and Tim Rice's Aida is a 1999 concept album that contains songs from, but predates the main production of, the 2000 musical "Aida".

Unbroken (Tim McGraw song) | "Unbroken" is a song written by Annie Roboff and Holly Lamar, and recorded by American country music artist Tim McGraw.  It was released in May 2002 as the fourth and final single from his album "Set This Circus Down".  The song reached No. 1 on the "Billboard" Hot Country Singles & Tracks (now Hot Country Songs) charts.

Telluride (song) | "Telluride" is a song originally recorded by Tim McGraw on his 2001 album "Set This Circus Down".  McGraw's version, though never a single, charted twice on Hot Country Songs as an album cut.  It reached No. 52 on its first entry, and then re-entered at No. 59 in 2002.

Elton John and Tim Rice's Aida

- Elton John related album title present (different work) — [5] — "Elton John and Tim Rice's Aida is a 1999 concept album"

Ambiguities:
- "Tiny Dancer" — passages show the song title but do not indicate which Elton John album it originally appears on; could map to "Tiny Dancer (song)" or various album/compilation pages.
- "Elton John album" — passages mention "Elton John and Tim Rice's Aida" [5], but that is a different work and does not resolve which Elton John album contained "Tiny Dancer".

Bridge / missing fact:
- The specific Elton John album that originally included the song "Tiny Dancer" (album title and release year) is not stated in the provided passages.

Retrieval direction:
- Open page "Tiny Dancer (song)" (Wikipedia) or search query: "Tiny Dancer Elton John which album"
- Open page "Madman Across the Water" (Elton John album) or search query: "Madman Across the Water track listing Tiny Dancer"
- If needed, open "Elton John discography" or search query: "Elton John albums 1971 Tiny Dancer"
Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets above.
hop2_docs: Tiny Dancer (Hold Me Closer) | "Tiny Dancer (Hold Me Closer)" is a song by Ironik, from his debut album "No Point in Wasting Tears", released on 11 May 2009.  The song heavily samples British singer Elton John's 1972 single "Tiny Dancer".  This is the first single by Ironik to be released without a maxi single.

Tiny Dancer | "Tiny Dancer" is a 1971 song by Elton John with lyrics by Bernie Taupin.  It appears on John's fourth album, "Madman Across the Water", and was released as a single in 1972.  In the U.S. it was certified Gold on 19 May 2005, and Platinum on 19 August 2011 by the Recording Industry Association of America.  In the UK, "Tiny Dancer" has been certified Silver by the British Phonographic Industry (BPI) for sales of 200,000 copies, despite never being released as an official single.

Tim McGraw and the Dancehall Doctors | Tim McGraw and the Dancehall Doctors is the seventh studio album by country musician Tim McGraw and the first to feature his band The Dancehall Doctors.  It was released in November 2002 and was recorded on a mountaintop studio in upstate New York.  Four singles were released.  Two songs were in the movie "Black Cloud", starring McGraw.  The album also included a cover of Elton John's "Tiny Dancer", which was released only to the AC format, although it also reached the country charts from unsolicited airplay.  The album debuted at number 2 on the Billboard 200 with first week sales of 602,000

Elton John: The Classic Years | Elton John: The Classic Years (known in North America as Elton John: The Remasters) was a remastering series of Sir Elton John's albums, in which some of the albums included bonus tracks.

Elton John albums discography | The albums discography of English musician/singer-songwriter Elton John consists of 30 studio albums, 4 live albums, 7 soundtrack albums, 16 compilation albums and 3 extended plays, as well as 2 other albums.  John has sold more than 200 million albums worldwide.  Elton John, is The Highest Selling British Solo Artist of All Time

Elton John's Greatest Hits Vol. 3 | Elton John’s Greatest Hits Volume III is the twenty-seventh album release for Elton John.  Released in 1987, 10 years after "Elton John's Greatest Hits Volume II", the compilation album features his greatest hits from 1979–1986 and was made available only in the United States and Canada.  All of the songs featured had previously been released on a previous album.

The Fox (Elton John album) | The Fox, released in 1981, is the fifteenth official album release for Elton John.  With sales of approx. 300,000 US copies, it was one of Elton's poorest-selling albums in the United States.
